{"path": "workspace/python_guide.txt", "content": "================================================================================
                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
  1.1  Code Style & Formatting (PEP 8)
  1.2  Project Structure
  1.3  Type Hints
  1.4  Error Handling
  1.5  Logging
  1.6  Virtual Environments & Dependency Management
  1.7  Documentation
  1.8  Performance & Idiomatic Python
  1.9  Security Best Practices

PART 2: PYTHON TESTING FRAMEWORKS
  2.1  Overview of Major Frameworks
  2.2  unittest (Standard Library)
  2.3  pytest (Most Popular Third-Party)
  2.4  nose2
  2.5  doctest
  2.6  Hypothesis (Property-Based Testing)
  2.7  tox & nox (Test Automation)
  2.8  Framework Comparison Table

PART 3: WRITING EFFECTIVE TESTS
  3.1  Test Structure (AAA Pattern)
  3.2  Fixtures & Setup/Teardown
  3.3  Mocking & Patching
  3.4  Parametrized Testing
  3.5  Code Coverage
  3.6  Continuous Integration Tips

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
------------------------------------
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (no tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Use blank lines to separate top-level functions/classes (2 lines) and
  methods inside a class (1 line).
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library-specific imports
- Avoid wildcard imports (from module import *).
- Naming conventions:
    - snake_case for functions, variables, and modules
    - PascalCase for classes
    - UPPER_CASE for constants
    - _leading_underscore for internal/private use
    - __double_underscore for name mangling (rarely needed)
- Use tools like black, ruff, or flake8 to automate style enforcement.
- Use isort to automatically sort imports.

1.2 PROJECT STRUCTURE
----------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern build config (or setup.cfg/setup.py)
    ├── requirements.txt        # Pinned dependencies
    ├── requirements-dev.txt    # Dev dependencies (testing, linting)
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── .env.example            # Environment variable template
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── conftest.py         # Shared pytest fixtures
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    └── docs/

- Use the "src layout" (src/mypackage) to prevent accidental imports of
  the package during development.
- Keep configuration in pyproject.toml (PEP 621 standard).

1.3 TYPE HINTS
---------------
- Use type hints (PEP 484) to improve code clarity and enable static analysis.
- Tools: mypy, pyright, or the built-in type checker in your IDE.

    def greet(name: str, age: int | None = None) -> str:
        if age is not None:
            return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."
        return f"Hello {name}!"

- Use Optional[X] or X | None (Python 3.10+) for nullable types.
- Use Union[X, Y] or X | Y for multiple types.
- Use Protocol classes for structural subtyping (duck typing with types).
- Use TypedDict for dictionaries with a fixed schema.
- Use Literal for constrained string/values.
- Run mypy as part of your CI pipeline.

- Tools: mypy, pyright, or the built-in type checker in your IDE.

    def greet(name: str, age: int | None = None) -> str:
        if age is not None:
            return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."
        return f"Hello {name}!"

- Use Optional[X] or X | None (Python 3.10+) for nullable types.
- Use Union[X, Y] or X | Y for multiple types.
- Use Protocol classes for structural subtyping (duck typing with types).
- Use TypedDict for dictionaries with a fixed schema.
- Use Literal for constrained string/values.
- Run mypy as part of your CI pipeline.

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
------------------------------------
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (no tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Use blank lines to separate top-level functions/classes (2 lines) and
  methods inside a class (1 line).
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library-specific imports
- Avoid wildcard imports (from module import *).
- Naming conventions:
    - snake_case for functions, variables, and modules
    - PascalCase for classes
    - UPPER_CASE for constants
    - _leading_underscore for internal/private use
    - __double_underscore for name mangling (rarely needed)
- Use tools like black, ruff, or flake8 to automate style enforcement.
- Use isort to automatically sort imports.

1.2 PROJECT STRUCTURE
----------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern build config (PEP 621)
    ├── requirements.txt        # Pinned dependencies
    ├── requirements-dev.txt    # Dev dependencies (testing, linting)
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── .env.example            # Environment variable template
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── conftest.py         # Shared pytest fixtures
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    └── docs/

- Use the "src layout" (src/mypackage) to prevent accidental imports of
  the package during development.
- Keep configuration in pyproject.toml (PEP 621 standard).

1.3 TYPE HINTS
---------------
- Use type hints (PEP 484) to improve code clarity and enable static analysis.
- Tools: mypy, pyright, or the built-in type checker in your IDE.

    def greet(name: str, age: int | None = None) -> str:
        if age is not None:
            return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."
        return f"Hello {name}!"

- Use Optional[X] or X | None (Python 3.10+) for nullable types.
- Use Union[X, Y] or X | Y for multiple types.
- Use Protocol classes for structural subtyping (duck typing with types).
- Use TypedDict for dictionaries with a fixed
